title: Reading a paycheck
deck: Gross pay is what you earned; net pay is what hits your bank account. Everything between them is a deduction. The first lesson of financial literacy is learning to read the gap.
objectives: Read a standard pay stub: distinguish gross pay from net pay, identify the major deductions (federal income tax, state income tax, FICA), and compute take-home pay as a percentage of gross.

content.text: You start a new job at $20 an hour, work 40 hours your first week, and mentally bank $800. Then payday arrives and your direct deposit hits the bank for $602.80. The missing $197.20 was not stolen; it is distributed across the various withholding columns of your pay stub. This gap between gross earnings and take-home pay is the single most universal piece of financial mathematics in adult life.

This chapter introduces the math of taxes and the stock market — the two financial-literacy domains that surround almost every other money decision a person will face. Sections 7.1 through 7.3 cover income taxes, beginning here with the structure of an individual paycheck. Section 7.2 introduces the progressive bracket math that determines federal income tax owed; Section 7.3 reconciles the year-end totals (withholding versus tax owed) on a Form 1040. Sections 7.4 through 7.6 turn to investing, covering stocks, bonds, and the diversification framework that ties them together.

Every paycheck begins with gross pay: the total dollar amount earned during the pay period before anything is withheld. Three standard deductions follow. The first is federal income tax withholding, an estimate of what the employee will eventually owe in federal tax, calibrated from Form W-4 and the IRS withholding tables. The second is state income tax in most states (a handful of states, including Texas, Florida, Nevada, and Washington, have no state income tax). The third is FICA, the Federal Insurance Contributions Act tax, which funds Social Security and Medicare. The employee FICA rate is fixed at 7.65%: 6.2% for Social Security plus 1.45% for Medicare. Employers pay a matching 7.65%, doubling the total amount flowing to those programs.

content.caption: Figure 7.1. A $1,000 gross paycheck visualized as a stack. Three deductions (federal income tax, FICA, state income tax) are subtracted in sequence, leaving $753.50 as the net pay actually deposited.

content.text: Definition 7.1. Gross pay is total earnings before any deductions. Net pay (or take-home pay) is the amount remaining after all deductions are subtracted: net = gross − deductions. The take-home percentage is net / gross, expressed as a percent.

Margin noteFICA is the largest single deduction for most low- and middle-income earners. At 7.65% on the employee side and 7.65% matched by the employer, more than 15% of total compensation goes to fund Social Security and Medicare benefits — programs that themselves account for nearly half of all U.S. federal spending. The Social Security portion (6.2%) is capped at an annual wage limit (around $168,600 in 2024); the Medicare portion (1.45%) has no cap.

A useful single number summarizing a paycheck is the take-home percentage: the ratio of net pay to gross pay, expressed as a percent. For a worker grossing $1,000 and netting $753.50, the take-home percentage is 753.50 / 1,000 = 75.35%. Typical take-home percentages for entry-level U.S. workers run between 75% and 85% of gross pay, with higher earners losing a larger share to federal withholding as their incomes move into the higher brackets discussed in Section 7.2.
The salary figure quoted in a job offer is not the number you can budget against. The take-home pay is.

Common mistakeBudgeting against gross pay. A job offer of “$50,000 a year” sounds like $50,000 to spend, but the take-home is closer to $40,000 in most states — about $3,300 a month rather than $4,167. Rent, car payments, and savings targets calibrated against the gross figure produce a chronic monthly shortfall. The honest planning number is net pay; the gross figure exists mainly for tax-reporting and salary-comparison purposes.

Example 7.1. A 40-hour weekly paycheck.

A worker earns $20/hour and worked 40 hours this week. Federal withholding is 12% of gross, state withholding is 5%, and FICA is the standard 7.65%. Compute the gross, the three deductions, the net, and the take-home percentage.

Solution.

Step 1. Gross = $20 × 40 = $800.

Step 2. Deductions are percentages of gross: federal = 0.12 × 800 = $96; FICA = 0.0765 × 800 = $61.20; state = 0.05 × 800 = $40. Total deductions = $197.20.

Step 3. Net = $800 − $197.20 = $602.80.

Step 4. Take-home percentage = 602.80 / 800 = 0.7535 = 75.35%.

About three-quarters of every gross dollar reaches the worker's account; the remaining quarter is split among the federal government, the state, and the FICA programs.

In the wildThe Form W-4 you complete on day one of a new job determines the federal-withholding line on every subsequent paycheck. The form translates filing status, dependents, and any extra withholding into a per-pay-period dollar amount that the employer remits to the IRS on your behalf. Adjusting the W-4 mid-year is a common move when life circumstances change: a marriage, a new child, a second job, or simply the realization that withholding is too high or too low. The IRS publishes a free Tax Withholding Estimator (irs.gov) that translates your situation into the precise W-4 entries needed to produce a near-zero refund — the financially optimal target.

content.problem: A part-time worker earns $15/hour and works 30 hours. Federal withholding is 10%, FICA is 7.65%, state is 4%. Compute her net pay (rounded to the nearest cent).
content.answer: $352.58. Gross = $450; total deductions = 21.65% × $450 = $97.43 (rounding); net = $450 − $97.43 = $352.57 (or $352.58 depending on rounding order).

content.problem: A salaried worker earns $65,000 per year and is paid biweekly (26 pay periods). Compute her gross pay per period.
What is the standard FICA rate withheld from an employee's paycheck, and what two programs does it fund?
An employee with $48,000 annual gross income takes home $36,720 per year. What is her take-home percentage?
True or false: state income tax is deducted from every U.S. employee's paycheck.
content.answer: False. A handful of states (including Texas, Florida, Nevada, Washington, and a few others) have no state income tax, so workers in those states see no state withholding on their stubs.

content.problem: Why is the take-home percentage typically lower for high-income earners than for low-income earners?
content.answer: The federal income tax is progressive (Section 7.2): higher incomes are taxed at higher marginal rates, so the federal-withholding share of each paycheck grows with income. FICA is a flat percentage (up to its wage cap), so the variation in take-home percentage is driven primarily by federal and state income tax.

content.problem: Lila's monthly gross is **$4,200**. Deductions: FICA 7.65%, federal withholding **$485**, state tax **$160**, health insurance **$180**. What is her net pay?

Marcus contributes **$300/month pre-tax to a 401(k)** from a gross of $4,500. Compared to no contribution, how does this change his **taxable income** for the month? Does FICA still apply to the $300?

content.title: Gross to net — one paycheck, line by line
content.problem: Your monthly **gross pay** is **$5,000**. From this, **FICA** takes **7.65%**, **federal withholding** takes **$650**, and **health insurance** is **$200/month**. What is your **net pay** (take-home)?
content.solution:
**Walk the deductions from gross.**

Gross: **$5,000**.

FICA: 0.0765 × 5,000 = **$382.50**.

Federal withholding: **$650** (this is pre-paid federal income tax).

Health insurance: **$200**.

Net pay = 5,000 − 382.50 − 650 − 200 = **$3,767.50**.

That's about **75%** of gross taking home. The remaining 25% went to taxes and benefits. Knowing this split is the foundation for any realistic budget.

content.title: Pre-tax deductions lower your taxable income
content.problem: Same job: monthly gross **$5,000**, FICA 7.65%, federal withholding $650. Now suppose you contribute **$500/month to a 401(k)**, which is **pre-tax**. How does this change your **taxable** income, and how does it change your **net pay** (roughly)?
content.solution:
**Pre-tax means the contribution happens before federal income tax is calculated.**

Original taxable income (for federal purposes): $5,000.

After 401(k) contribution: 5,000 − 500 = **$4,500 taxable**.

Federal withholding was calibrated against the higher taxable income; it would drop accordingly (rough estimate: by about $500 × 22% bracket ≈ **$110/month**). New withholding ≈ $540.

*Approximate new net pay:*

5,000 (gross) − 382.50 (FICA, still on full gross) − 500 (401k) − 540 (lower federal) − 200 (health) ≈ **$3,377.50**.

*The takeaway.* Net pay dropped by only **~$390**, not the full $500 you contributed. The $110 difference is the **immediate tax savings** that a pre-tax retirement account provides — and that money still belongs to you, just locked into the 401(k).

content.title: Summary
content.content: Gross pay is what you earn; net pay is what you keep. The gap is filled by FICA, federal withholding, state tax, and any pre-tax benefits (401(k), HSA, health insurance). Pre-tax contributions are the most efficient because they reduce both your taxable income and your tax bill at the same time.

content.title: Reading a Gen Z pay stub
content.content: First-job pay stubs are a rite of passage. "Why is my take-home so much less than my salary?" The answer lives on the stub itself. For a $50K annual salary, the math typically lands at: gross $4,167/month → minus FICA $319 → minus federal withholding ~$400 → minus state tax → minus health insurance → take-home about **$3,200/month**. That **~25% gap** between gross and net is the federal/state/FICA tax wedge, and understanding it is what separates a confused first-job hire from one who can actually budget their take-home.

content.text: The federal-withholding line on every paycheck is an estimate; whether that estimate is right is settled on April 15.
